title: W5 C Properties
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW‚ÄìSydney



  Week 5C    ‚Äî    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/8
 Continuity and Other Properties




                                  2/8
                 Continuity and Differentiation


Proposition 1

 If f : I ‚Üí R has a derivative at x, then f is continuous at x.




                                                                  3/8
                     Continuity and Differentiation


 Proposition 1

    If f : I ‚Üí R has a derivative at x, then f is continuous at x.


Proof    We need to show that limu‚Üíx f (u) = f (x). Equivalently, we show
limu‚Üíx |f (u) ‚àí f (x)| = 0.




                                                                            3/8
                     Continuity and Differentiation


 Proposition 1

    If f : I ‚Üí R has a derivative at x, then f is continuous at x.


Proof    We need to show that limu‚Üíx f (u) = f (x). Equivalently, we show
limu‚Üíx |f (u) ‚àí f (x)| = 0.
  ‚ó¶ For any u Ã∏= x, we have
                                        f (u) ‚àí f (x)
                    |f (u) ‚àí f (x)| =                 |u ‚àí x|.
                                            u‚àíx




                                                                            3/8
                     Continuity and Differentiation


 Proposition 1

    If f : I ‚Üí R has a derivative at x, then f is continuous at x.


Proof    We need to show that limu‚Üíx f (u) = f (x). Equivalently, we show
limu‚Üíx |f (u) ‚àí f (x)| = 0.
  ‚ó¶ For any u Ã∏= x, we have
                                        f (u) ‚àí f (x)
                    |f (u) ‚àí f (x)| =                 |u ‚àí x|.
                                            u‚àíx

  ‚ó¶ Taking limits on both sides of the expression,

                                     f (u) ‚àí f (x)
        lim |f (u) ‚àí f (x)| = lim                  lim |u ‚àí x|.
        u‚Üíx                    u‚Üíx       u‚àíx       u‚Üíx




                                                                            3/8
                      Derivatives are Informative

 Proposition 2

   Let I ‚äÜ R be an open interval. Suppose f has a derivative at x ‚àà I.
   (a) If Df (x) > 0, then there exists Œ¥ > 0 such that f is strictly
        increasing on (x ‚àí Œ¥, x + Œ¥).
   (b) If Df (x) < 0, then there exists Œ¥ > 0 such that f is strictly
        decreasing on (x ‚àí Œ¥, x + Œ¥).


                                                 Df (x)

                                                    f




Proof   See suggested exercises.

                                                                         4/8
                      The Useful Product Rule

Suppose both f and g are differentiable at x ‚àà R.
  ‚ó¶ One can show that h = f + g is also differentiable at x, and further
                        Dh(x) = Df (x) + Dg(x).




                                                                           5/8
                      The Useful Product Rule

Suppose both f and g are differentiable at x ‚àà R.
  ‚ó¶ One can show that h = f + g is also differentiable at x, and further
                         Dh(x) = Df (x) + Dg(x).

  ‚ó¶ What if h = f √ó g?




                                                                           5/8
                      The Useful Product Rule

Suppose both f and g are differentiable at x ‚àà R.
  ‚ó¶ One can show that h = f + g is also differentiable at x, and further
                         Dh(x) = Df (x) + Dg(x).

  ‚ó¶ What if h = f √ó g?


 Proposition 3

   Let f and g be two real-valued functions defined on an open interval
   I ‚äÜ R. If f and g are differentiable at x, then so is the product
   function h = f √ó g. Moreover,

                  Dh(x) = Df (x) g(x) + f (x) Dg(x).




                                                                           5/8
                       The Useful Product Rule



Proof    Since h = f √ó g and g are both continuous at x, we have

      h(u) ‚àí h(x)       f (u)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(x) ¬± f (x)g(u)
lim               = lim
u‚Üíx      u‚àíx        u‚Üíx              u‚àíx




                                                                   6/8
                       The Useful Product Rule



Proof    Since h = f √ó g and g are both continuous at x, we have

      h(u) ‚àí h(x)       f (u)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(x) ¬± f (x)g(u)
lim               = lim
u‚Üíx      u‚àíx        u‚Üíx               u‚àíx
                                                                        
                          f (u)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(u)    f (x)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(x)
                  = lim                         +
                    u‚Üíx           u‚àíx                      u‚àíx




                                                                             6/8
                       The Useful Product Rule



Proof    Since h = f √ó g and g are both continuous at x, we have

      h(u) ‚àí h(x)       f (u)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(x) ¬± f (x)g(u)
lim               = lim
u‚Üíx      u‚àíx        u‚Üíx               u‚àíx
                                                                        
                          f (u)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(u)    f (x)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(x)
                  = lim                         +
                    u‚Üíx           u‚àíx                      u‚àíx
                                                                              
                                f (u) ‚àí f (x)                        g(u) ‚àí g(x)
                   = lim                      g(u)       + lim f (x)
                      u‚Üíx           u‚àíx                    u‚Üíx          u‚àíx




                                                                                     6/8
                       The Useful Product Rule



Proof    Since h = f √ó g and g are both continuous at x, we have

      h(u) ‚àí h(x)       f (u)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(x) ¬± f (x)g(u)
lim               = lim
u‚Üíx      u‚àíx        u‚Üíx               u‚àíx
                                                                        
                          f (u)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(u)    f (x)g(u) ‚àí f (x)g(x)
                  = lim                         +
                    u‚Üíx           u‚àíx                      u‚àíx
                                                                              
                                f (u) ‚àí f (x)                        g(u) ‚àí g(x)
                   = lim                      g(u)       + lim f (x)
                      u‚Üíx           u‚àíx                    u‚Üíx          u‚àíx

                   = Df (x)g(x) + f (x)Dg(x).




                                                                                     6/8
                     The Dreaded Chain Rule

Proposition 4

 Let f : I ‚Üí R and g : J ‚Üí R, where I and J are open subsets of R
 and f (I) ‚äÜ J. If f is differentiable at x ‚àà I and g is differentiable
 at y = f (x) ‚àà J, then the composite function h = g ‚ó¶ f is
 differentiable at x and satisfies

                      Dh(x) = Dg(f (x)) Df (x).




                                                                          7/8
                       The Dreaded Chain Rule

 Proposition 4

   Let f : I ‚Üí R and g : J ‚Üí R, where I and J are open subsets of R
   and f (I) ‚äÜ J. If f is differentiable at x ‚àà I and g is differentiable
   at y = f (x) ‚àà J, then the composite function h = g ‚ó¶ f is
   differentiable at x and satisfies

                        Dh(x) = Dg(f (x)) Df (x).


Proof   Notice

         g(f (u)) ‚àí g(f (x))   g(f (u)) ‚àí g(f (x)) f (u) ‚àí f (x)
                             =                                   .
                u‚àíx               f (u) ‚àí f (x)        u‚àíx

Now let u ‚Üí x and use the continuity of f at x.




                                                                            7/8
                       Higher Order Derivatives


Suppose f : I ‚Üí R admits a derivative everywhere in the open interval I.
  ‚ó¶ If so, f is said to be differentiable on I.




                                                                           8/8
                       Higher Order Derivatives


Suppose f : I ‚Üí R admits a derivative everywhere in the open interval I.
  ‚ó¶ If so, f is said to be differentiable on I.

We can treat Df itself as a function defined on I.
  ‚ó¶ If Df is continuous on I, f is said to be continuously differentiable.




                                                                             8/8
                       Higher Order Derivatives


Suppose f : I ‚Üí R admits a derivative everywhere in the open interval I.
  ‚ó¶ If so, f is said to be differentiable on I.

We can treat Df itself as a function defined on I.
  ‚ó¶ If Df is continuous on I, f is said to be continuously differentiable.

  ‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative at x ‚àà I, refer to it as the second derivative
    of f at x and write D2 f (x).




                                                                               8/8
                       Higher Order Derivatives


Suppose f : I ‚Üí R admits a derivative everywhere in the open interval I.
  ‚ó¶ If so, f is said to be differentiable on I.

We can treat Df itself as a function defined on I.
  ‚ó¶ If Df is continuous on I, f is said to be continuously differentiable.

  ‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative at x ‚àà I, refer to it as the second derivative
    of f at x and write D2 f (x).

  ‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative everywhere in the interval I, say f is twice
    differentiable on I.




                                                                               8/8
                        Higher Order Derivatives


Suppose f : I ‚Üí R admits a derivative everywhere in the open interval I.
  ‚ó¶ If so, f is said to be differentiable on I.

We can treat Df itself as a function defined on I.
  ‚ó¶ If Df is continuous on I, f is said to be continuously differentiable.

  ‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative at x ‚àà I, refer to it as the second derivative
    of f at x and write D2 f (x).

  ‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative everywhere in the interval I, say f is twice
    differentiable on I.

Treat D2 f itself as a function defined on I . . .




                                                                               8/8
